Zillow claims this due to its extensive database that includes input from real homeowners as well as their sophisticated home valuation model in the Zestimate.

Zillow believes its ZHVI has distinct advantages over the Case-Shiller index. The Case-Shiller (CS) Index uses the repeat-sales methodology, meaning it measures price changes by looking at homes that have resold in a region, while the ZHVI only includes homes that have sold at least twice in recent history and excludes all new construction.

Here are a few charts pulled directly from a piece by Zillow comparing the two indexes. We tried to get this data to do our own analysis, but the research group there would not provide it. Here’s a link to that article.

© Zillow.com

Another difference between the CS and ZHVI is that CS includes foreclosure resales. Zillow emphasizes that foreclosure resales are “substantially different from non-distressed sales” and are often considerably discounted from such. Zillow claims that using foreclosure resales represents “two very different market segments”. Eric Stubbs, Ph.D. at RBC WM believes foreclosure sales are a matter of taste and says that foreclosures are actual sales, so they do, in fact, affect the price of surrounding properties.

In any case, the ZHVI is another tool in the tool belt to determine home values and is useful due to its timeliness and variety of information.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include: iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Pan American Silver: Profit Outlook Surges As Silver Breaks Higher
data.description:
The price of silver has broken to the upside since the beginning of the year, rising by around 26% to over $33 per ounce. For years, investors and analysts, including myself, have viewed silver as an ideal hedge against inflation risk associated with excessively dovish monetary policy. The gold-to-silver ratio has also been at a historical extreme, creating a value opportunity for silver that seems to be closing. Silver also has a relatively high industrial use demand growth, arguably in a growing physical shortage, led by higher conductor demand for electronic and energy systems.

Silver ETFs like (SLV) are a means to invest in silver close to the spot price. However, many investors prefer not to invest in commodity ETFs, instead opting for direct physical possession or investing in silver miners. The largest silver mining stock by market capitalization is Pan American Silver Corp. (NYSE:PAAS) with a market capitalization of $10B. The company will soon grow significantly larger after it completes the acquisition of MAG Silver (MAG), purchasing the stock at a substantial premium of ~25%. Of course, because silver has risen by over 10% since early May, the incomplete acquisition has arguably already paid off.

Pan American is a huge silver company with diversified operations across the Americas. Its purchase of MAG will increase its production in North America and potentially lower its costs per ounce, given MAG's primary mine has an all-in sustaining cost of just $12 per equivalent ounce sold, with around 19 million ounces in production. Pan American's 2024 AISC was ~$19 with 21 MOZ production and a staggering 468M in proven and probable reserves. PAAS also pays a dividend of ~$0.40 per share, a low 1.4% yield, but more than many mining stocks.

Given the acquisition and the sharp rise in silver, investors are likely curious if PAAS may soon rise to a new all-time high. Indeed, a significant silver breakout should benefit large silver miners, such as PAAS, more than silver investment funds. That said, despite its vast size, PAAS has underperformed the underlying commodity for some time, potentially discouraging investment despite its potential for profit growth.

A Look At Silver Mining Cost Inflation

The fundamental issue I've seen in both silver miners and gold is their exposure to cost growth. Silver and gold mining are extremely energy and labor-cost-intensive because mine yields are relatively low. A great deal of ore must be extracted and processed for a small amount of precious metals. Silver and gold are fundamentally hedges against inflation, but if inflation pushes labor, fuel, and capital costs up faster, their incomes will fall.

Pan American's operating margin is strongly correlated with the price of silver; however, a higher silver price has been necessary for its margins to be positive. Its margins are elevated today, but are not significantly higher than they've been over the past decade, despite silver reaching decade highs in the most recent consecutive quarters. See below:

In 2019, Pan American Silver reported a silver segment AISC of around $11. Today, it is 72% higher at about $19. The consumer price index has risen by 27% since early 2019, while the price of silver has increased by about 120%. Thus, the net change is an increase in profits, but only on the ~28% net rise in silver relative to the increase in its AISC, which is growing far faster than inflation at around 10% per year since 2019.

Although PAAS is approximately 64% more volatile than the silver ETF (SLV), its beta to SLV is around 1.1, given its correlation of about 0.68. PAAS outperformed SLV sharply in 2020 when silver prices initially spiked during the Fed's considerable quantitative easing effort. However, as global central bank easing efforts exacerbated supply-shock inflation, silver production costs rose similarly, pushing PAAS's relative performance back down. Overall, PAAS has performed about the same as SLV and the silver miner ETF (SIL) since before 2020:

Notably, the peaks at troughs in PAAS's relative performance to SLV generally align with those of the relative performance of silver to energy commodities. Silver miners are not a hedge against inflation because, in general, we can expect inflation to push the price of all commodities higher. Since silver mining is primarily energy-intensive, silver miners' margins can be associated with the relative price of silver to fuel inputs. Interestingly, the silver-to-energy price ratio is elevated again today:

The elevation in this index points to higher profit margins for PAAS in the immediate future. Indeed, Pan American reported an AISC far below guidance and expected levels in Q1 at just $13.94 for its silver segment. Fuel prices were historically low in Q1, with crude falling to around its lowest level since the pandemic lockdown crash. Notably, labor costs in key production areas, such as Mexico, have declined. Mexico and most Latin American nations have experienced relatively high labor cost growth in recent years, despite having extremely low unemployment and decent GDP growth. Mexico's business confidence trend is increasingly bearish, indicating a slowing economy that may result in less labor cost inflation for Pan American. This trend is also reflected in Argentina's economy, which has come down from the extreme inflation of years past.

PAAS Profit Outlook and Valuation

The company's immediate profit outlook is robust compared to TTM levels due to the combined decrease in cost factors and rises in silver and gold. Since its acquisition is not yet complete, I will project based only on Pan American's metrics, but note that the MAG deal is attractive mainly due to silver price appreciation since the deal was priced.

At the beginning of the year, Pan American looked to silver production of around 20.5M ounces and gold of around 770K ounces. Its AISC forecast was $16.25 to $18.25, with gold's cost forecasted at $1.52K to $1.63K per ounce. Its Q1 performance generally beat cost expectations with lower AISCs. Though oil is higher today, there is a general lag between energy costs and production costs, so for now, I'll assume costs remain at the low end of the projected range.

Annualizing, Pan American's expected silver revenue for the next year is likely around $745M (current price times production guidance), while gold is higher at $2.6B. The company's gold output has increased, making it less of a pure-play silver company, but one that benefits greatly from the ongoing gold bull market.

At today's market prices and the low-end of Pan American's cost guidance, we can expect it to generate around $20 per ounce of operating profit per silver ounce mined and ~$1.86K per ounce of gold. The net segment operating income should be around $410M for silver and ~$1.43B from gold, or $1.84B combined.

AISC figures do not include depreciation, growth CapEx, exploration, financing cost, income taxes, and one-off cost or gain factors. Pan American's TTM operating income is $530M, but its cash from operations is higher at $837M due to the impact of depreciation. The company also had $54M in net interest costs. Accounting for interest and taxes, I estimate Pan American's net operating cash flow (adjusted for sustaining CapEx) at around $1.25B, well-above its TTM CFO of ~$840M.

Thus, its forward price-to-cash flow may be as low as 8.4 times, potentially leaving it undervalued. Of course, my assumption is based on precious metals sustaining their recent gains while costs remain at the low end of the expected range. The recent rise in oil prices associated with the Israel-Iran conflict could undermine that, potentially dramatically if oil prices rapidly rise over $100, as I suspect.

The Bottom Line

Arguably, the prices of silver and gold are rising in anticipation of monetary distress in the US and other key banking countries. Extremely high debt burdens, higher borrowing costs, and legislation that increases deficits accelerate a potential government financing crisis. For years, I've maintained the view that the US cannot ease its debt burden without having sustained high inflation. Furthermore, Trump's efforts to encourage the Federal Reserve to ease monetary policy may exacerbate inflation in the immediate future.

Gold and, to a lesser extent, silver are expensive compared to interest rates after expected inflation. The silver and gold rally may be a bubble, or it may be a fundamental sign of an emerging inflationary debt crisis. The risk of an oil price shock associated with Iran could exacerbate this issue, mirroring the inflationary wave of the 1980s following the Iranian oil crisis. If so, the Federal Reserve's ability to manage higher inflation and the economy will be compromised, as the Fed is unable to reduce the impact of supply-side inflation without harming economic demand.

If we look at immediate figures, PAAS is potentially undervalued. The company is poised to generate excellent cash flows, given that precious metals prices are rising while AISCs are not, a trend that contrasts with the experience over the past five years. However, in the long run, there is no fundamental reason why costs will not rise proportionally to inflation. I am bullish on PAAS due to the rise in silver and gold, and it may be a good short-term investment. However, I prefer SLV and GLD if we assume an investment horizon greater than a year because I expect energy costs to rise as much, and potentially more than, the price of precious metals. I think PAAS is a decent bet today, but its most significant risk may be a large increase in energy costs should my concerns regarding the oil market become a reality.
